1: ûpəlištîm nilḥāmîm ʾel yiśrāʾēl wayyānusû ʾanšê yiśrāʾēl — "the Philistines attacked Israel, and the Israelites fled." The verb nûs (H5127, to flee/escape) marks total military collapse; this is not a fighting retreat but a rout. Wayyippəlû ḥălālîm bəhar haggILBŌʿA — "falling slain on Mount Gilboa." The battle is narrated in five verses with the economy of catastrophe.
2: wayyadbiqû pəlištîm ʾet šāʾûl wəʾet bānāyw — "the Philistines closed in hard on Saul and his sons." Dāḇaq (H1692, to cling/press hard against) — a pursuit verb implying the Philistines are faster and closing. Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchishua are named — all three sons die. Jonathan's death is noted without elaboration here; 2 Sam 1:17-27 provides the full lament.
3: wayyiḵbad hamməlḥāmāh ʾel šāʾûl — "the battle pressed hard against Saul" (kāḇēd, H3513, to be heavy — the same root as kāḇōd/honor; "weighty" battle). Wayyimṣāʾuhû hammôrîm ʾănāšîm baqqešet wayyāḥel məʾōd méhammôrîm — "the archers found their mark, and he was badly wounded" (ḥûl, H2342, to writhe/be in severe pain — often used of birth pangs). Saul is hit and incapacitated before making the decision of v4.
4: ʾāḇāh / dāqar — "Draw your sword (šəlōp ḥarbaḵā) and run me through (dāqar, H1856, to stab/thrust through)." Saul's motive: pen yāḇōʾû hāʿărēlîm hāʾēlleh ûdqārûnî wəhiṯʿallǝlû ḇî — "lest these uncircumcised men come and abuse me." Hithʿallel (Hithpael of ʿālal, H5953, to act against/abuse/mock) — the fear is public dishonor rather than death itself. Wayyimāʾēn — "but his armor-bearer refused" (māʾēn, the same refusal-verb used in ch28:23 and ch15). Saul then falls on his own sword — wayyiqaḥ šāʾûl ʾet haḥereb wayyippôl ʿāleyhā — the self-destruction of the king is narrated in one clause.
5: wayyarʾ nōśēʾ kēlāyw kî mēt šāʾûl wayyippôl gam hûʾ ʿal ḥarəbô — "when the armor-bearer saw that Saul was dead, he too fell on his own sword and died with him." The armor-bearer's death is briefly noted without commentary; his act is one of ancient honorable solidarity — the servant follows the master into death. The pairing of their deaths — master, then servant — frames Saul's end as comprehensively isolated from divine support.
6: wayyāmat šāʾûl ûšəlōšet bānāyw wənōśēʾ kēlāyw gam kol ʾănāšāyw bəyôm hahûʾ yaḥdāw — "so Saul died, along with his three sons, his armor-bearer, and all his men — all on that same day (yaḥdāw)." Yaḥdāw (together/at once) provides the temporal closure: the Saulide dynasty ends in a single day, as Samuel prophesied (māḥār in 28:19). The word "together" (yaḥdāw) is the same word used for the equal sharing in 30:24 — but there it described communal solidarity; here it describes communal annihilation.
7: wayyaʿazbû ʾet hehārîm wayyānusû — "they abandoned their cities and fled." The Israelite withdrawal from the Jezreel Valley and Transjordan follows the military collapse. Wayyāḇōʾû pəlištîm wayyēšəḇû ḇāhēm — "the Philistines came and settled in them." Philistine settlement of Israelite towns is the immediate geopolitical consequence; 2 Samuel opens in this situation.
8: wihyî mimmmāḥărāt — "the next day." Philistine plundering of the dead was standard ancient military practice. Wayyimṣəʾû ʾet šāʾûl wəʾet šəlōšat bānāyw nōpəlîm bəhar haggILBŌʿA — "they found Saul and his three sons fallen on Mount Gilboa." The verb māṣāʾ (to find) — a discovery, not a pursuit. Saul is now an object to be found rather than a king who ruled.
9: wayyəḇaśśərû — "to announce the victory" (bāśar, H1319, Piel, to bring good news / announce glad tidings). The root of bəśōrāh (good news/gospel) in its Hebrew form. The Philistines "evangelize" their gods (ʿăṣabbêhem) and people with Saul's death. The irony that the death of Israel's king is proclaimed as "good news" in pagan temples echoes the later NT inversion: the death of Israel's true King is proclaimed as good news to all nations. Wayyāsîrû ʾet rōʾšô wəʾet kēlāyw — head removed and armor stripped; the head would be displayed as a trophy (cf. David's display of Goliath's head, 17:54).
10: wayyāśîmû ʾet kēlāyw bêt ʿaštārôt — "they placed his armor in the temple of Ashtaroth." Ashtaroth was the Canaanite goddess of war and fertility, associated with the Philistine pantheon. Dedicating captured armor to a deity was standard ancient Near Eastern victory practice — the spoil belongs to the god who gave the victory. Saul's armor ends as a votive offering; the man who was to be YHWH's covenant king becomes a trophy in a pagan sanctuary.
11: The people of Jabesh-gilead hear (šāmᵉʿû) what the Philistines did — the hearing that leads to action. Jabesh-gilead is the city Saul rescued in his first military act as king (ch11, from Nahash the Ammonite). The loyalty they display now is covenant-gratitude (ḥesed is the word 2 Sam 2:5 uses for their deed).
12: wayyāqumû kol ʾîš ḥayil wayyēlǝḵû kol hallaylāh — "all their fighting men rose, traveled through the night." The night march mirrors Saul's night journey to Endor (28:8). But where Saul traveled in darkness to seek an illegal oracle, the men of Jabesh-gilead travel in darkness to perform a loyal burial. Wayyiśrəpû ʾōtām šām — "burned them there." Cremation was unusual in Israelite practice; this may reflect the advanced state of decomposition or Philistine mutilation, making burial of intact bodies impossible.
13: wayyiqəḥû ʾet ʿaṣmōtêhem wayyiqbərû taḥat hāʾēšel — "they gathered the bones and buried them under the tamarisk tree (ʾēšel, H815)." ʾĒšel is the tamarisk — a tree associated with significant events (Abraham planted one at Beersheba, Gen 21:33; Saul sat under one at 22:6). The burial under a significant tree is a dignified interment for a fallen king. Wayyāṣumû šiḇʿat yāmîm — "they fasted for seven days." Seven-day mourning is the full period of intensive grief (cf. Job 2:13). The men of Jabesh-gilead honor Saul with the complete mourning rite — an act of covenant loyalty that will be explicitly commended by David in 2 Sam 2:5-6.
